agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship, enabling multi-recipient shipments. This is a fundamental structural change to a core aggregate that impacts data models, external integrations, billing, routing, and operational workflows.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core context responsible for shipment lifecycle, recipient information, and delivery coordination.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods moving through the delivery network from origin to one or more destinations. The root aggregate entity."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party designated to receive a delivery within a Shipment. Has a name, address, phone number, and delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contactless delivery)."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A Shipment with more than one Recipient, where each Recipient may have distinct addresses and delivery preferences."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"The first or default Recipient on a Shipment — critical for backward compatibility with systems expecting a single recipient."},{"term":"Shipment Leg","definition":"A segment of a multi-recipient shipment's journey between consecutive delivery stops. Each leg terminates at a Recipient's address."},{"term":"Delivery Status","definition":"The fulfillment state of a delivery. In a multi-recipient model, this must be tracked per-Recipient, not just per-Shipment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment with zero Recipients is logically meaningless — there is no delivery destination. Allowing it would corrupt the shipment lifecycle.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to create a Shipment with zero Recipients, or to remove the last Recipient from an existing Shipment, must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum data required for a courier to execute a delivery. Without them, a delivery cannot physically occur.","testable_assertion":"Persisting or updating a Recipient with a null/empty name, address, or phone number must be rejected by validation."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment may have independent delivery preferences.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Delivery preferences are a business policy feature, not a data integrity constraint. A Recipient without explicit preferences can fall back to shipment-level defaults.","testable_assertion":"Given a Shipment with two Recipients, setting a signature-required preference on Recipient A must not affect Recipient B's preferences."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Shipment-level status must be derived from the aggregate of per-Recipient delivery statuses.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment cannot be marked 'delivered' while one of its Recipients has not received their delivery. Misrepresenting aggregate status corrupts downstream reporting and billing.","testable_assertion":"A Shipment with 3 Recipients where 2 are delivered and 1 is pending must report its status as 'partially delivered' (or equivalent), not 'delivered'."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Existing single-recipient shipments must remain fully functional and queryable without data migration altering their business semantics.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"2 million production records must not have their meaning changed. A migration that subtly alters semantics (e.g., wrapping a recipient in an array but losing implicit 'primary' designation) breaks auditability and partner contracts.","testable_assertion":"After migration, querying any pre-existing Shipment must return the same recipient name, address, and phone as before the model change."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"External partner API contracts must continue to function for single-recipient shipments without requiring immediate partner-side changes.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual and business relationship constraint, not a data integrity invariant. It can be relaxed when partners are ready to migrate.","testable_assertion":"An API call to fetch a single-recipient Shipment in the legacy format must return a response identical in structure and content to the pre-change API response."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"For multi-recipient shipments, recipient delivery order must be explicitly defined or derivable.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Route optimization may reorder deliveries, but the system must know intended order. This is operational policy, not a hard data constraint.","testable_assertion":"A multi-recipient Shipment must expose a delivery sequence, and reordering Recipients must be an explicit operation."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The creator specifies two or more Recipients with different addresses and delivery preferences","then":"The Shipment is persisted with all Recipients, each retaining their individual address and preferences, and a delivery sequence is assigned."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment created before the model change","when":"It is retrieved via the API","then":"It returns the original Recipient data with no loss of information, and the response is backward-compatible with the legacy single-recipient schema."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment where Recipient A has been delivered but Recipient B has not","when":"The Shipment status is queried","then":"The Shipment-level status reflects a partial delivery state (not 'delivered'), and each Recipient's individual status is available."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An external partner integration consuming shipment data","when":"A single-recipient Shipment is sent to the partner API","then":"The payload matches the existing partner contract format exactly, requiring no partner-side code changes."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment is being created","when":"The creator attempts to save it with zero Recipients","then":"The system rejects the request with a clear validation error."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment with three Recipients","when":"The second Recipient is removed","then":"The Shipment retains the remaining two Recipients, the delivery sequence is updated, and the Shipment remains valid."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A Shipment with two Recipients","when":"Delivery preferences are updated for Recipient A only","then":"Recipient B's preferences remain unchanged, and the Shipment reflects the update only for Recipient A."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"All Recipients on a multi-recipient Shipment share the same address","expected_behavior":"The system must allow this (e.g., multiple packages to the same building for different people) but should not collapse them into a single Recipient. Each Recipient remains a distinct entity."},{"scenario":"A Recipient is removed from a Shipment after their delivery has already been completed","expected_behavior":"The system must reject the removal or retain a historical record. Deleting a delivered Recipient would corrupt delivery history and invoicing."},{"scenario":"External partner receives a multi-recipient Shipment but only supports single-recipient format","expected_behavior":"The system must either send one message per Recipient or send only the primary Recipient, depending on the partner's integration contract. Silent data loss (dropping extra Recipients) is unacceptable."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is duplicated/cloned for operational reasons","expected_behavior":"All Recipients and their individual preferences must be copied. A clone that copies only the first Recipient would silently lose data."},{"scenario":"Billing for a multi-recipient Shipment","expected_behavior":"Pricing model must account for multiple stops. A multi-recipient Shipment billed as a single-stop delivery would be a revenue loss. Conversely, billing each Recipient as a separate Shipment would overcharge."},{"scenario":"Partial delivery failure — one Recipient's address is unreachable","expected_behavior":"The Shipment must not be marked as failed overall. Remaining Recipients should still be deliverable. The failed Recipient enters a retry or exception workflow independently."},{"scenario":"Concurrent updates to different Recipients on the same Shipment","expected_behavior":"Optimistic concurrency at the Shipment aggregate level must prevent conflicting writes, but the system should not reject non-overlapping Recipient updates unnecessarily."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-recipient shipments introduce new pricing complexity. Revenue opportunity from consolidated shipments (one pickup, multiple drops), but risk of revenue leakage if billing models don't account for per-stop costs. Migration costs for partner integrations could be significant.","operational":"Route optimization must now handle multi-stop shipments. Dispatch, tracking, and proof-of-delivery workflows all become per-Recipient rather than per-Shipment. Operational dashboards and SLA tracking need redesign to handle partial delivery states. Data migration of 2M records requires careful planning.","user":"Customers gain flexibility to send to multiple recipients in one transaction. However, tracking UX becomes more complex — a customer viewing shipment status must understand partial delivery states. Notification design must be per-Recipient.","risk":"High risk of breaking external partner integrations if backward compatibility is not rigorously maintained. Data migration of 2M production records carries corruption risk. The aggregate status derivation logic (BR-4) is a common source of bugs. Domain model change touches the core aggregate, so blast radius is large.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End customers (senders)","impact":"New capability to send to multiple recipients per shipment. Must understand new tracking and notification model."},{"group":"End customers (recipients)","impact":"Delivery preferences become per-recipient. Must receive individual notifications and tracking links."},{"group":"External partner integrations","impact":"Must maintain backward compatibility or coordinate migration. Partners consuming single-recipient format will break without an adapter layer."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Route planning, driver assignments, and delivery workflows must handle multi-stop shipments. Training and tooling changes required."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Pricing models must be updated for multi-stop shipments. Invoicing and revenue recognition affected."},{"group":"Customer support","impact":"Support agents need updated tools and training to handle partial delivery states, per-recipient issues, and more complex shipment structures."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Core aggregate redesign with data migration. API versioning strategy required. All downstream consumers of the Shipment model must be audited."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The scenario is well-defined with clear intent (1:1 to 1:N relationship change) and known constraints (2M existing records, external partner APIs). Confidence is not higher because several important details are unspecified: the exact structure of delivery preferences, the partner API contract details, the current billing model, and whether 'multi-recipient' means multiple drops from one package or multiple packages in one shipment.","high_confidence_areas":["The core invariant that a Shipment must have at least one Recipient","Backward compatibility requirement for existing data and partner APIs","Per-Recipient status tracking necessity","Data migration risk for 2M records"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact definition of 'delivery preferences' — what options are included is unspecified","Whether multi-recipient means multi-drop (one vehicle, sequential stops) or multi-package (split at hub)","Partner API contract specifics — how many partners, what format, what SLAs for migration","Billing model implications — per-stop pricing vs flat rate vs distance-based","Whether a Shipment aggregate boundary should expand to include Recipients or if Recipients should become their own aggregate with eventual consistency"],"assumptions":["Multi-recipient means multiple delivery destinations (multi-drop), not multiple packages to the same address","Existing partner APIs use a flat single-recipient JSON structure, not already an array","The 2M existing records can be migrated with a non-destructive additive schema change","Delivery preferences are per-Recipient, not per-Shipment, as stated in the requirement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The term 'delivery preferences' is introduced without definition. It could mean time-window preferences, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contactless delivery, or all of these. The scope of this concept materially affects the Recipient entity design and validation rules.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","End customers (recipients)"],"options":["Define a fixed set of preference types now and extend later","Treat preferences as a flexible key-value bag","Defer preference design and ship multi-recipient with address-only first"],"recommendation":"Define a minimum viable set of delivery preferences (e.g., time window, signature required, delivery instructions) before implementation begins. This prevents over-engineering while ensuring the model is grounded in real business needs."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"It is unclear whether a multi-recipient Shipment represents one physical package visiting multiple stops sequentially, or multiple packages consolidated under one shipment ID and potentially split at a hub. These are fundamentally different logistics operations with different routing, billing, and tracking implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Dispatch","Finance / Billing","Engineering"],"options":["Multi-drop model: one vehicle, sequential delivery stops","Multi-package model: packages may split and travel independently","Support both models as sub-types of multi-recipient Shipment"],"recommendation":"Clarify with the product team immediately. If multi-drop, the Shipment remains a single routing unit. If multi-package, each Recipient effectively needs independent tracking and the domain model is significantly more complex."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The backward compatibility strategy for external partner APIs is unspecified. It is unclear whether partners should receive multi-recipient data in a new format, receive only the primary recipient in the old format, or receive one API call per recipient.","affected_stakeholders":["External partner integrations","Engineering"],"options":["API versioning: v1 returns single-recipient format, v2 returns multi-recipient","Adapter layer: always flatten to single-recipient for legacy partners","Per-partner configuration: each partner opts into new or old format"],"recommendation":"Implement API versioning with a v1 compatibility layer that returns the primary Recipient in the legacy format. Partners migrate to v2 on their own timeline."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship, enabling multi-recipient shipments. This is a fundamental structural change to a core aggregate that impacts data models, external integrations, billing, routing, and operational workflows.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core context responsible for shipment lifecycle, recipient information, and delivery coordination.","ubiq

content.thinking: Let me analyze this domain model evolution from single-recipient to multi-recipient shipments.
